problems.title: Sudan famine & displacement crisis
problems.blurb: Sudan's civil war has produced the world's largest displacement and hunger emergency, with confirmed famine and a 2026 UN response plan funded at under 16% of the ~$9bn needed.
problems.discoveredFrom: UN/OCHA Global Humanitarian Overview 2026 and Stimson Center both flag Sudan as the largest yet most under-funded and media-marginalised crisis globally.

clean-water.summary: As of the 2025 WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme update, 2.1 billion people — 1 in 4 globally — still lack safely managed drinking water, including 106 million who drink untreated surface water. Progress is real but slow: 961 million people gained safely managed services between 2015 and 2024, lifting global coverage from 68% to 74%, with rural coverage rising from 50% to 60%. The gap is concentrated in low-income countries, fragile/conflict settings, and rural, indigenous and minority communities. The strongest evidence base is for point-of-use water treatment: a meta-analysis of 18 RCTs found chlorination cut all-cause under-5 mortality by roughly 24%, at about USD 40 per DALY averted — on par with the most cost-effective child-health interventions like vaccination.

interventions.title: Point-of-use and in-line chlorination
interventions.evidence: A University of Chicago meta-analysis of 18 RCTs (NBER w30835) found water treatment, driven by chlorination, reduced all-cause child mortality ~24% at roughly USD 40 per DALY averted.
interventions.actor: Health ministries, water utilities and NGOs (e.g. Evidence Action's Dispensers for Safe Water)

interventions.title: Chlorine dispensers at collection points
interventions.evidence: The same meta-analysis estimated USD 28–72 per DALY averted for point-of-collection dispensers and free chlorine programs, ~45x below the 1x-GDP cost-effectiveness threshold.
interventions.actor: Local governments and community water committees

interventions.title: Piped, safely managed supply expansion
interventions.evidence: WHO/UNICEF JMP 2025 credits the 68%→74% coverage rise partly to expanded managed piped systems, the durable route out of surface-water reliance.
interventions.actor: National water utilities with World Bank / development-bank finance

interventions.title: WASH investment in fragile and rural contexts
interventions.evidence: WHO/UNICEF 2025 identify low-income, fragile, rural and indigenous populations as facing the widest disparities, requiring targeted funding.
interventions.actor: Donors, UN agencies and national governments

malaria-tb-hiv.summary: These three treatable diseases still kill well over 2 million people a year, concentrated in low-income countries. Malaria caused an estimated 610,000 deaths and 282 million cases in 2024 (about 9 million more cases than the prior year), with ~95% of deaths in the WHO African Region and 11 countries carrying two-thirds of the burden. Tuberculosis remains the leading single-agent infectious killer, with 1.23 million deaths in 2024 (including 150,000 among people with HIV) and a record 8.2 million people newly diagnosed. People living with HIV are about 12 times more likely to develop TB, making integrated HIV/TB care essential. Proven tools exist and are scaling — since 2000 an estimated 2.3 billion malaria cases and 14 million deaths have been averted — but artemisinin partial resistance is now confirmed or suspected in at least 8 African countries, threatening the gains.

interventions.title: Insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs)
interventions.evidence: A 2024 cost-effectiveness review (1Day Sooner / MMV) found ITNs the most economical malaria-prevention method in Africa, narrowly ahead of the R21 vaccine.
interventions.actor: The Global Fund, PMI and national malaria programs

interventions.title: R21/Matrix-M and RTS,S malaria vaccines
interventions.evidence: Both cut malaria cases >50% in year one; R21 costs ~USD 3.90/dose vs ~USD 10.90 for RTS,S with comparable efficacy, per WHO and Serum Institute data.
interventions.actor: Gavi, WHO and health ministries in endemic countries

interventions.title: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention combined with vaccination
interventions.evidence: A Lancet Infectious Diseases phase-3 trial (Burkina Faso/Mali) showed vaccine plus SMC cut clinical malaria, severe disease and deaths ~75% versus either alone.
interventions.actor: National programs with Global Fund / Unitaid support

interventions.title: TB case-finding, treatment and preventive therapy
interventions.evidence: WHO's TB fact sheet reports a record 8.2 million people diagnosed in 2023–24; scaling diagnosis and treatment is the main lever against the 1.23 million annual deaths.
interventions.actor: WHO End TB program and national TB programs

interventions.title: Integrated HIV/TB care and antiretroviral therapy
interventions.evidence: WHO notes people with HIV are ~12x more likely to develop TB and TB is their leading cause of death; co-management and ART sharply cut mortality.
interventions.actor: PEPFAR, The Global Fund and national HIV programs

air-pollution.summary: Air pollution is one of the largest environmental health risks, associated with about 7 million premature deaths a year from combined ambient and household exposure; a UNICEF/HEI State of Global Air analysis attributed 8.1 million deaths to air pollution in 2021, making it the second-leading risk factor for death, including for children under five. More than 90% of these deaths trace to fine particulate matter (PM2.5), which penetrates the lungs and bloodstream; roughly 68% of outdoor-pollution deaths are from ischaemic heart disease and stroke. The strongest recent evidence is that household fuel transitions drive large exposure and mortality reductions: switching from biomass to LPG in India cut kitchen PM2.5 ~93% and personal exposure ~78%, and China's shift away from household solid fuels was the dominant factor in its 2005–2015 PM2.5 mortality decline, with clean-fuel switching estimated to avert on the order of half a million deaths a year.

interventions.title: Household clean-cooking (LPG) transition
interventions.evidence: The HAPIN trial in India recorded a sustained ~93% drop in kitchen PM2.5 and ~78% drop in personal exposure among women switching from biomass to LPG.
interventions.actor: National energy ministries (e.g. India's PMUY) with WHO and clean-cooking funds

interventions.title: Phasing out household and small-scale coal
interventions.evidence: A PNAS study found household-fuel change dominated China's 2005–2015 PM2.5 exposure and mortality decline; clean-fuel switching could avert ~500,000 deaths/year.
interventions.actor: City and provincial governments (e.g. Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei coal bans)

interventions.title: Tighter ambient air-quality standards toward WHO guidelines
interventions.evidence: WHO estimates meeting interim target 1 (35 µg/m³ PM2.5) would save ~300,000 lives a year worldwide.
interventions.actor: National environment agencies enforcing WHO Air Quality Guidelines

interventions.title: Cleaner transport and industrial emission controls
interventions.evidence: WHO attributes ~68% of outdoor-pollution deaths to heart disease and stroke driven largely by traffic and industrial PM2.5, targetable via fuel and emission standards.
interventions.actor: Transport and industry regulators, city clean-air zones
